_description: Three-column comparison of policy competences: EU today (TFEU Articles 2–6, factual/citable), a federal reference model (US Constitution / CRS, factual/citable), and a federal EU (operator's proposed allocation — [PLACEHOLDER]).

_sources.col_a: Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union (TFEU), Articles 2–6. EUR-Lex: https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A12016E%2FTXT
_sources.col_b: US Constitution, Article I §8; Congressional Research Service, 'Federalism, State Sovereignty, and the Constitution' (2020). https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/R/R45323

a.text: Exclusive EU competence for eurozone members (19/27 states). Non-euro states retain national monetary policy.

b.text: Federal. Congress has exclusive power to coin money and regulate its value. Federal Reserve is a federal institution.

c.text: Federal. Exclusive federal competence over currency and monetary policy for all member states. The ECB operates as a full federal reserve.

a.text: No EU army. Common Foreign and Security Policy (CFSP) and Permanent Structured Cooperation (PESCO) operate by unanimity — effectively intergovernmental. No common defence budget.
a.cite: TEU Art. 42–46; TFEU does not classify defence as shared competence

b.text: Federal. Congress declares war, raises armies, and appropriates defence funds. President is Commander-in-Chief. States may not maintain warships.
b.cite: US Const. Art. I §8 cl. 11–16; Art. II §2

c.text: Federal. A unified European Armed Forces under federal command, funded by the federal budget. Member states may retain national guards for domestic emergencies.

a.text: CFSP operates by unanimity in the European Council and Council. No binding EU foreign policy without unanimous agreement. Member states retain independent foreign-policy capacity.

b.text: Primarily federal. President conducts foreign affairs; Senate ratifies treaties. States may not enter treaties with foreign governments.
b.cite: US Const. Art. II §2; Art. I §10

c.text: Federal. The federal executive represents the Union internationally and negotiates all treaties. Member states cannot conduct independent foreign policy.

a.text: No EU-level direct taxation. EU budget funded by 'own resources' (customs duties, VAT-based contribution, GNI-based contribution). Direct and indirect tax harmonisation requires unanimity.
a.cite: TFEU Art. 110–113; Council Decision 2020/2053 on own resources

b.text: Concurrent. Federal government levies income, corporate, payroll, and excise taxes. States levy their own income, sales, and property taxes independently.
b.cite: US Const. Art. I §8 cl. 1; 16th Amendment

c.text: Concurrent. The federal government levies corporate and income taxes directly to fund the federal budget. States retain independent taxing powers.

a.text: Exclusive EU competence. Common commercial policy and customs union are exclusive EU competences. Common market is regulated by EU institutions.

b.text: Federal. Congress regulates commerce with foreign nations and among the states. States may not impose tariffs on interstate commerce.
b.cite: US Const. Art. I §8 cl. 3 (Commerce Clause)

c.text: Exclusive federal competence. A fully completed single market with unified commercial policy and no internal barriers.

a.text: Shared competence. EU sets binding targets (e.g., EU Green Deal, Fit for 55 package); member states implement. EU Emissions Trading System is EU-level.

b.text: Concurrent. Federal EPA sets standards; states may exceed federal minimums (California waiver model). Congress has not enacted binding federal climate legislation as of 2022.
b.cite: Clean Air Act §177; EPA authority under Massachusetts v. EPA (2007)

c.text: Concurrent. Federal government sets binding floor standards and carbon pricing. Member states may enact stricter environmental protections.

a.text: Shared competence. Schengen Area abolishes internal borders for participating states; external border managed by Frontex under EU mandate. Asylum rules partially harmonised (Dublin Regulation).

b.text: Federal. Congress has plenary power over immigration and naturalisation. States cannot set immigration law, though they may limit benefits to non-citizens.
b.cite: US Const. Art. I §8 cl. 4; Arizona v. United States (2012)

c.text: Federal. Exclusive federal control over external borders, immigration, and asylum policy. Complete freedom of movement internally.

a.text: No EU-level fiscal policy. EU budget is ~1% of EU GNI — insufficient to act as a macroeconomic stabiliser. SGP/TSCG constrain national deficits. Next Generation EU (€750bn, 2021–2026) is a temporary, one-off instrument.

b.text: Federal government maintains a substantial fiscal capacity (~24% of GDP). Federal automatic stabilisers (unemployment insurance, Medicaid, food stamps) act counter-cyclically across states.

c.text: Federal. A substantial federal budget (e.g., 10-15% of GDP) with borrowing capacity, capable of macroeconomic stabilisation and counter-cyclical investment.
